finding: Participant expressed uncertainty about whether the upload process they were about to attempt would work correctly, showing lack of confidence in the pathway despite having identified a potential solution.
observed_signal: Participant described a plan to use 'My Documents' for uploading and then used keyboard navigation (control home, arrow down) to locate it, but prefaced the attempt with hedging language about not having explored it much and uncertainty about whether they could 'figure out how to upload a file.'
stated_signal: I haven't really explored in there too much, but there's a way of uploading extra stuff... and I'm gonna have a go at right now... see if I can figure out how to upload a file.
rationale: The participant identified a potential pathway ('My Documents') but expressed uncertainty about whether they could successfully execute the upload action. Both stated signal (hedging with 'if I can figure out') and observed signal (cautious navigation) align on confidence friction. This is brief hesitation before attempting the task, fitting S5 and L2 (moderate friction, non-optimal framing but task proceeds).
structural_amplification_note: Keyboard navigation combined with uncertain knowledge of feature location may amplify confidence friction for screen reader users or those unfamiliar with the application structure.

finding: Participant discovered that uploading documents to 'my documents' does not make them visible to the application, creating confusion about the feature's purpose.
observed_signal: Participant uploaded a test document to 'my documents' and then noted that the upload did not make the document visible.
stated_signal: You can attach a document to an existing application from here, but just uploading something to my documents doesn't make it visible, which I think is interesting.
rationale: The participant expected uploading to 'my documents' would make the document visible to the application, but the interface behaved differently. The stated observation ('which I think is interesting') and subsequent clarification ('it acts as a repository') show the participant understood the actual behaviour after the fact, indicating brief confusion followed by recovery. This is unexpected behaviour friction with quick reconciliation.

finding: Participant expressed mild confusion about the structural design of course information tables, noting that headings appear both outside and inside tables.
observed_signal: Participant navigated using keyboard shortcuts (Control+Home, H for headings), encountered course tables with dual heading structures, and paused to comment on the design pattern.
stated_signal: That's a little interesting. We've got headings outside the tables, but then in the tables there's also headings.
rationale: The participant identified an unusual information structure and verbalized a mild observation without expressing frustration or impediment. Navigation continued successfully and the participant moved on to select a different heading option ('Living in Queensland'). This is a brief, low-impact comprehension observation that does not block task progress.
structural_amplification_note: Assistive-technology reliance (keyboard navigation via headings) may make structural inconsistencies in table markup more salient to screen reader users, though no actual access failure was demonstrated in this window.

finding: Participant was unaware they remained on the document upload page and had to manually navigate away, creating a brief disorientation.
observed_signal: Participant said 'I didn't realize that I hadn't changed the page' and then used control+home to navigate, explicitly switching pages to the resources link.
stated_signal: I didn't realize that I hadn't changed the page but that's OK.
rationale: The participant was uncertain about their current location and had to manually correct course. Both observed behaviour (manual navigation away) and stated signal (acknowledgement of the oversight) indicate wayfinding confusion. Recovery was immediate and the participant expressed mild acceptance ('but that's OK'), placing this at medium friction with quick recovery.

finding: Participant experienced uncertainty about whether the file upload was successful due to lack of visual confirmation and had to verify by checking the documents table.
observed_signal: Participant completed the upload action, observed a page refresh, expressed confusion about whether the action succeeded, then navigated to the documents table to verify the upload was stored.
stated_signal: I saw the page refresh and I'm like wait hang on, did I do something wrong, did it work? And I had to go into the documents table and there was the extra row and it had the uh document upload test document in there. That's the only reason.
rationale: The participant completed the upload task but experienced confidence friction—uncertainty about whether the action succeeded. Both the stated confusion ('did I do something wrong, did it work?') and the observed workaround (checking the documents table) indicate wayfinding/confirmation uncertainty. The participant recovered by self-verification, matching S5 and L2 (brief hesitation, one non-optimal path, task completed).

finding: Participant identified a missing upload button as a design gap that would improve confidence and clarity about submission intent.
observed_signal: Participant described the current drag-and-drop or browse workflow and explicitly suggested an upload button would be beneficial.
stated_signal: What I think would have been cool is if there was an actual upload button so you know that your next key press is going to send the file.
rationale: The participant observed unexpected behaviour—the absence of an explicit upload button creates ambiguity about when the submission occurs. The stated suggestion ('so you know that your next key press is going to send the file') indicates expected vs. actual design divergence. The tone is matter-of-fact suggestion rather than frustration, and the task was completed, placing this at S6 and L1 (minor issue with negligible impact on outcome).
structural_amplification_note: For keyboard-navigating users (as stated by the participant), the absence of an explicit, labeled upload button increases cognitive load in understanding the submission trigger point.

finding: Participant identified confusion in the form layout where multiple fields and categories are arranged on the same line in the visa section, making it difficult to parse the intended structure.
observed_signal: Participant paused and explained the layout problem: 'if you've applied for an Australian visa before, it gives you three options, like 3 spaces, and on each line you have all of the different categ like all of the different things that you're supposed to answer.'
stated_signal: that part is confusing because it's all on the same line.
rationale: The participant expressed clear confusion about how the form content is organized visually. The stated difficulty ('confusing') and observed identification of the layout problem both point to comprehension friction caused by unclear information structure. The participant was able to articulate the issue without being blocked from proceeding, indicating recoverable friction at the medium level.

finding: Participant expressed concern that the form's single-line layout forces users to choose between accessibility and visual formatting, creating uncertainty about whether the form has been filled correctly.
observed_signal: Participant described a hypothetical scenario of filling out the form and explained the cognitive burden of worrying whether line breaks have disrupted the form's visual appearance.
stated_signal: If you type a bit of information, you hit new line, and then the next part of the form ends up being on its own line and you're never sure if you've messed up the overall look of the form, at least that's what goes through my mind.
rationale: The participant clearly articulated confusion about form structure and layout expectations. The stated uncertainty about whether formatting has been disrupted matches comprehension friction—the participant understands the mechanism but is unsure whether the resulting form structure is correct. This is recoverable hesitation without task blockage.
structural_amplification_note: Participants relying on keyboard entry or non-standard text input methods experience structurally amplified friction when form layouts do not accommodate accessible formatting (e.g., line breaks, spacing) without creating ambiguity about correctness.

finding: Participant expressed uncertainty about the meaning and purpose of form field dollar amounts and their relationship to the enrollment process.
observed_signal: Participant paused while reading form fields aloud, then explicitly stated confusion about what the dollar amounts refer to and whether they are spaces to type financial figures.
stated_signal: I'm not sure what they refer to or whether they are, um, just spaces to type financial figures.
rationale: The participant encountered unclear labeling or explanation of financial form fields and voiced comprehension friction. The stated confusion and hesitation indicate a noticeable but recoverable friction event where the participant did not fully block task progress but expressed genuine uncertainty about field purpose.

finding: Participant identified poor formatting and labeling in form fields that negatively impacts assistive technology readability, specifically multiple questions and options placed on the same line with identical labels.
observed_signal: Participant reviewed and verbally analyzed the form structure, noting that questions about visa status, family relationships, and dependent children all had formatting issues where multiple fields and options were grouped on the same line with duplicate labels.
stated_signal: The formatting on this one is not very helpful to assistive tech... you've got 12345 and the date of birth... all of the questions are on the same line... they're all labeled the same... potential for 4 family members and you can put relationship, visa status.
rationale: The participant directly stated and observed multiple accessibility friction points where form layout violates assistive technology requirements: questions crammed on single lines, duplicate labels for different fields, and numeric/date sequences presented without proper structure. This is a significant component failure that would substantially impede screen reader users, matching S3 (significant component failure) and L3 (significant friction with struggle evident in analysis).
structural_amplification_note: This friction is structurally amplified for blind and assistive technology users, who rely on clear label-to-input mapping and sequential field navigation. The duplicate labels and single-line grouping would make it extremely difficult to programmatically determine which label applies to which field.

finding: Participant identified that form layout with mixed fields on single lines creates confusion about data associations and relationships.
observed_signal: Participant described experiencing difficulty with form layout where visa details and children details appeared on the same line, making it unclear which information belonged to which record.
stated_signal: Having all of that on one line. And then the next line is like moving on to the next child or the next visa or something like that, makes it really hard to figure out what belongs where.
rationale: The participant's stated difficulty and observed description both indicate comprehension friction caused by unclear visual formatting and layout structure. The friction is recoverable and the participant completed the task, but expressed noticeable frustration about the clarity of form structure.

finding: Participant encountered an unexpected interaction with the submit button that forced app closure and restart, though the task was eventually completed.
observed_signal: Participant hit the submit button, ended up having to close the app and reopen it, but then successfully enrolled in MFA.
stated_signal: I don't know whether that was a bug in like hitting the submit button or whether I was meant to just hit enter in the edit box, uh, cos I ended up having to close the app and reopen it, but anyhow, it worked.
rationale: The participant's observed behaviour and stated uncertainty both indicate the interface responded unexpectedly to the submit button interaction, requiring app restart. Although the task was completed, the unexpected behaviour and forced recovery path constitute a significant component failure that was worked around, matching S3 and L3 (significant friction, struggle, task completed after recovery).

finding: Participant expressed uncertainty about the location of the feedback entry point and stated a need to search for it, indicating wayfinding confusion.
observed_signal: Participant used control-home to go to the top of the page and announced a plan to search systematically from top to bottom, then bottom to top, to find the feedback entry point.
stated_signal: I have in theory read where the feedback link is, but I haven't actually gone exploring so this will be interesting. I've no idea where that's gonna be, so I'm gonna start at the top, and if I can't find anything at the top by arrowing down, I'm gonna go to the bottom and arrow up.
rationale: The participant stated clear uncertainty about the location of the feedback link ('I've no idea where that's gonna be') and adopted a systematic search strategy. This is wayfinding friction—the participant understands what they need to do but is uncertain where to find it. The matter-of-fact tone and the fact that the participant initiates a deliberate search strategy without visible frustration places this at S5 (brief hesitation, recoverable) and L2 (moderate friction with non-optimal path).

finding: Participant identified that the feedback link is placed in an awkward location at the bottom of the page and is presented by NVDA as a confusing string without clear separation.
observed_signal: Participant described navigating to the end of the page and then back up to locate the feedback link, and noted NVDA's rendering as unclear.
stated_signal: I found a feedback link, but that is found in the most awkward spot. You have to go all the way down the page. And then you have to go up the page... NVDA presents it as like a whole string. You're not really sure what's going on.
rationale: The participant experienced accessibility friction due to poor link positioning and assistive-technology rendering issues. While the participant eventually found the link and could proceed, the stated confusion and awkward navigation path represent noticeable but recoverable friction typical of S5 and L2.
structural_amplification_note: Screen-reader users (NVDA) experience structural amplification due to link presentation as an undifferentiated string, making it harder to distinguish the feedback link from surrounding footer content compared to sighted users.

finding: Participant recognized the feedback link location as suboptimal and noted it could be harder to find if they hadn't known about it beforehand.
observed_signal: Participant located the feedback link at the bottom of the page and proceeded to click on it, but first commented on its placement and mentioned they might have clicked contact us instead.
stated_signal: It's not in the most uncommon place that I've ever found it, like sometimes you look at the very bottom of a page and there's like a give feedback link. Um, but I think to have a testing website and to have it all the way down there is a little interesting. It can make it a little harder to find, but knowing it's there, like definitely helped. If I didn't know that there was a feedback link, I actually think I would've clicked on the contact us link.
rationale: The participant expressed wayfinding uncertainty about the feedback link placement; they understood where it was but noted it could be harder to discover and acknowledged they might have selected a different link if unaware. This is mild confidence friction with quick recovery since they successfully located and accessed the link. The participant's matter-of-fact tone and successful completion without actual blocking place this at S5/L2.
structural_amplification_note: Participant is using NVDA (screen reader), which places multiple footer links on the same line, potentially amplifying discovery friction for AT users navigating by heading or link structure.

finding: Participant became disoriented while navigating the page structure and lost track of their task objective mid-navigation.
observed_signal: Participant used arrow keys to navigate page headings, identified several sections (applications, documents, resources, account, sign out), then stated they had forgotten their train of thought and were unsure where they were going.
stated_signal: I sort of just forgot my train of thought there. I wasn't really sure where I was going with that.
rationale: The participant exhibited wayfinding uncertainty and confusion about their next action after navigating the page structure. Both observed disorientation and stated explicit loss of direction indicate confidence friction. The participant recovered by deciding to continue with a keyboard action (control end), suggesting brief hesitation rather than full task blockage, fitting S5 and L2 gates.
structural_amplification_note: Keyboard-only navigation with heading structure may amplify disorientation for users relying on assistive technology, as complex page hierarchies can make mental models harder to maintain during linear heading traversal.

finding: Participant encountered strange behavior with combo boxes in the enrollment portal that prevented normal interaction.
observed_signal: Participant attempted to fill out a form and reported issues with combo box controls in the enrollment portal.
stated_signal: Strange behavior with combo boxes in the enrollment portal. NVDA doesn't [complete statement unclear].
rationale: The participant explicitly identified malfunction of combo boxes in the enrollment portal and mentioned NVDA (a screen reader), indicating accessibility friction. The incomplete statement and description of 'strange behavior' suggests the controls did not respond as expected, causing material difficulty. This represents significant friction with assistive technology integration.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user (NVDA) encountered combo box accessibility issues, which structurally amplifies friction for blind and low-vision users relying on keyboard navigation and announcements.

finding: Screen reader user reports that NVDA does not announce most options in combo boxes on the postgraduate enrollment form, making navigation inaccessible.
observed_signal: Participant stated that NVDA screen reader does not announce most options in combo boxes; participant suggested replicating the enrollment form and standardizing combo box format.
stated_signal: NVDA screen reader doesn't announce most options. In the combat. Most options in the combo boxes that are part of the, Um, post-grad enrollment form would be enrollment you can replicate the enrollment form. It would be helpful to make them a little little more standardized.
rationale: The participant, using NVDA assistive technology, explicitly identified that combo box options are not announced by the screen reader, creating a significant accessibility barrier. This prevents independent navigation of the enrollment form for blind or low-vision users. The participant's suggestion to standardize the format reflects frustration with a structural accessibility gap that requires a workaround or redesign.
structural_amplification_note: Blind and screen-reader users experience a structural barrier: combo boxes on the postgraduate enrollment form are not properly labeled or announced, rendering them inaccessible via keyboard and assistive technology. This creates a complete pathway blockage for this cohort.

finding: Screen reader user identified accessibility friction on a combo page and noted that labeling should be improved for assistive technology users.
observed_signal: Participant, identified as a screen reader user, articulated specific accessibility concern about combo page labeling and accessibility readiness.
stated_signal: As a screen reader user, it would be helpful to make them a little more silly at the combo page to make A D E R I S E D D A N D E R.
rationale: The participant identified an accessibility barrier on the combo page related to screen reader navigation and labeling. The stated concern reflects a known accessibility friction point affecting assistive technology users. While the participant did not abandon the task and provided constructive feedback, the friction is real and documented.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users experience friction navigating insufficiently labeled combo controls; this is a structural accessibility issue amplifying friction for AT-dependent users.

finding: Participant identified that the feedback entry point is not positioned prominently and could easily be missed by users.
observed_signal: Participant reflected on the location of feedback mechanisms across different parts of the UQ website, noting non-obvious positioning.
stated_signal: the only thing I can really say about feedback and like, um, finding it is that it's not in the most obvious position. It's something that could easily be missed.
rationale: The participant expressed uncertainty about the visibility and discoverability of the feedback mechanism, indicating wayfinding friction. The feedback form was ultimately located and deemed 'fine', suggesting the task was recoverable but the non-obvious positioning created noticeable hesitation and concern. This aligns with S5 (recoverable friction with brief confusion) and L2 (hesitation with task completion).

finding: Participant identified that the website's white background and lack of built-in dark mode or accessibility controls will force reliance on color inversion, which breaks image legibility and reduces the site to text-only.
observed_signal: Participant examined the menu, looked for accessibility features, and scanned the page footer for accessibility options without finding them.
stated_signal: Nothing in there about accessibility or anything like that. The website pretty much just becomes text for me. Pictures will be inverted, they won't look correct, so that kind of makes that null and void.
rationale: The participant, an assistive technology user with visual accessibility needs, identified a critical design gap: no dark mode or built-in accessibility controls force a workaround (color inversion) that degrades the experience by rendering images unusable. Both observed behaviour (checking menu and footer) and stated concern show significant friction on a core accessibility requirement. This meets S3 (significant barrier worked around) and L3 (struggle and frustration with accessibility-dependent task).
structural_amplification_note: Blind or low-vision users relying on high-contrast modes, dark mode, or colour inversion are structurally disadvantaged by white-background designs without accessible alternatives. The absence of accessibility controls and options creates a compounding barrier for this cohort.

finding: Participant reported that the website has no accessibility features and that color inversion renders images as unrecognizable blobs, creating a barrier to visual content consumption.
observed_signal: Participant inverted colors to improve contrast, then described the resulting display as cluttered with pictures appearing as strange blobs of color.
stated_signal: There's no accessibility at all. All of the pictures are um just weird, kind of strange looking blobs of color.
rationale: The participant is a user relying on color inversion for accessibility, and the interface fails to render images legibly under this mode. Both stated frustration ('no accessibility at all') and observed barrier (inability to interpret images) demonstrate significant accessibility friction that impedes task progress, though the participant continues searching.
structural_amplification_note: This friction is structurally amplified for users with visual processing needs or color vision deficiencies who rely on color inversion or high-contrast modes. The product fails to maintain image intelligibility under accessible color schemes.

finding: Participant expressed frustration that the website design expects users to figure out navigation independently rather than guiding them toward information pathways.
observed_signal: Participant clicked on the Master of Business program and began reviewing course details (entry requirements, duration, fields of study, program highlights).
stated_signal: It doesn't feel like it wants to navigate you towards something, it wants you to. It wants to figure out how smart you are and see if you can figure it out for yourself.
rationale: The participant expressed uncertainty and frustration about the navigation model, indicating confidence friction. However, they successfully located and began exploring the Master of Business program, suggesting the friction was noticeable but recoverable. The stated complaint aligns with the observed behaviour of independently searching and selecting a course.

finding: Participant found the website navigation and language confusing, perceiving it as designed for current university students rather than prospective postgraduate students.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through the menu, encountered menu glitch ('something strange was going on within in the menu'), located 'Find a program' and 'Masters' options, then critiqued the overall layout and messaging.
stated_signal: It feels like it is laid out and worded in a way that it's for university students, it doesn't seem like it's for people who are interested in going to university, it feels like it's for people who are already at university, it's not straightforward, it's not kind of easy, it doesn't entice you or want you to.
rationale: The participant expressed clear comprehension friction: the content and messaging language are unclear and misaligned with the prospective student persona. The stated criticism ('laid out and worded in a way that it's for university students') directly reflects confusion about who the content is designed for. The participant navigated through multiple sections but ultimately found the messaging uninviting and non-transparent, creating significant friction that materially impedes the prospective student's ability to proceed confidently.

finding: Participant expressed that the website layout and navigation are not intuitive despite the information being present and findable.
observed_signal: Participant navigated the page, located course information including fees and delivery methods, but then stated the navigation was not as intuitive as expected.
stated_signal: you just have to kind of dig around and and and and figure out where it is, um, it's not as intuitive as I thought it would be. Uh, in terms of the, the wording and the, and the way you navigate around the website.
rationale: The participant successfully found the information (fees, delivery methods, course overview) but expressed uncertainty and frustration about the navigation and layout structure. The stated friction about intuitiveness combined with observed task completion indicates medium friction—a noticeable but recoverable issue where the task outcome was not at risk.

finding: Participant perceived the postgraduate course discovery process as unnecessarily long and convoluted, feeling the website design prioritizes institutional verification over user accessibility.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through 'Study with us', 'How to apply', 'Choose type of study', 'Postgrad', and 'Choose your program' sequentially, then made a critical statement about the process.
stated_signal: This is um a lot longer winded than I thought it was gonna be. Um, It's uh yeah, again, it it feels like it's designed to make sure that you're a uni student before you can navigate through this website.
rationale: The participant completed multiple sequential steps but expressed frustration about excessive effort and poor navigation design. The stated complaint about the process being 'longer winded' and the observed multi-step pathway both indicate excessive effort friction. The participant recovered and continued, but the significant friction and explicit frustration place this at S4 and L3.

finding: Participant found the postgraduate course search interface unintuitive and required zoom to read small text in terms and conditions.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through Postgrad Search, Business section, and application workflow, then stated they needed to zoom in to read the small writing.
stated_signal: It's not intuitive at all. The writing's really small so it's actually quite hard, so it means that I've gotta zoom in to read what's what's actually going on.
rationale: The participant expressed confusion about the interface intuitiveness and explicitly identified small text as a comprehension barrier requiring workaround (zoom). This is moderate friction that required extra effort to resolve but did not block task completion.

finding: Participant experienced difficulty finding the registration or application entry point on the postgraduate courses page.
observed_signal: Participant narrated searching for 'How to apply', then 'start application', then 'Register', finally stating 'Finally we found it.'
stated_signal: How to apply. Uh, here you go, a start application. Gee, that, uh, oh, OK. Um Register, there we go. Finally we found it.
rationale: The participant took multiple attempts to locate the registration entry point, cycling through different labels or sections before succeeding. The relieved tone ('Finally we found it') confirms prior difficulty, but recovery was achieved without external help, matching medium friction.

finding: Participant made a typing error while entering email address and expressed frustration about the corrective effort required.
observed_signal: Participant typed the email address incorrectly, realized the mistake, and began re-entering it character by character.
stated_signal: I spelled that wrong. This is gonna be annoying.
rationale: The participant made a typo in the email field and stated explicit annoyance about the effort required to correct it. While the task is recoverable and the error is user-caused rather than product-caused, the expressed frustration about the excessive corrective effort and the fact that autofill was available but rejected indicates friction in the form of tedious manual entry. Severity is S5 because the friction is noticeable and creates mild negative emotion but does not block task completion.

finding: Participant identified that the font weight was too light and difficult to read without zooming, creating accessibility friction.
observed_signal: Participant commented on the thin, lightly weighted font and noted it does not stand out well against the background.
stated_signal: It's just not ideal. With a little bit of a heavier weighted font, I wouldn't have to zoom in, it's quite a thin, it's like a, a lightly weighted font, so it doesn't really stand out against the background well.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated that the thin font requires zooming to read and does not contrast well against the background. This is an accessibility friction issue with contrast and readability. The participant recovered by accepting terms and proceeding, indicating noticeable but recoverable friction matching the L2 gate.
structural_amplification_note: Users with visual impairments or low vision would experience amplified friction from insufficient font weight and poor contrast.

finding: Participant expressed frustration with the login/signup process and perceived the website as inaccessible, clinical, and not user-friendly for prospective students.
observed_signal: Participant logged in as Dave Johnson, found a program, and then described the interface as clinical, cold, and lacking accessibility features.
stated_signal: A sign in, uh a sign up is, is really annoying... it's not very accessible... it's just very clinical, it's very cold, it's not user friendly.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated the interface is not accessible and does not do anything on the front end to support accessibility. The observed behaviour shows the participant did complete the login and find a program, but the stated criticism and tone indicate noticeable friction with accessibility and design approach. This is a moderate friction event where the task progressed despite dissatisfaction.
structural_amplification_note: Users relying on assistive technology or keyboard navigation would likely experience amplified friction given the participant's specific observation that the website 'doesn't do anything to be accessible.'

finding: Participant perceived the website design and tone as misaligned with prospective students' needs, describing it as 'already made for university students' rather than those exploring options.
observed_signal: Participant reviewed the program page and critiqued the overall design approach and user positioning.
stated_signal: It seems like it's already made for university students, it's not for people who are just interested.
rationale: The participant expressed comprehension that the content and tone are misaligned with the audience of prospective students exploring options. The stated signal directly indicates a comprehension friction around messaging and positioning. This is noticeable friction but did not block task completion; the participant recovered and continued to evaluate the program.

finding: Participant reported inability to read content on the page due to small text size and expressed concern about accessibility.
observed_signal: Participant stated they cannot read what is on the screen and mentioned text being tiny; they also noted there is nothing in terms of accessibility.
stated_signal: I can't read, that is, that is going on... there's nothing in terms of accessibility here at all yet... everything's so small, they're tiny.
rationale: The participant explicitly cannot read the content due to small text size and identified a lack of accessibility features. This is an accessibility friction event where the interface does not meet accessible text sizing or zoom requirements. The participant's frustration and multiple expressions of the problem (repeated 'nothing' statements) indicate significant friction with multiple failed recovery attempts.
structural_amplification_note: This friction is structurally amplified for users with low vision or who rely on assistive magnification. The participant's avoidance of pinch-zoom due to fear of missing form elements during form completion further constrains accessible interaction patterns.

finding: Participant expressed frustration about form interaction patterns that require pinch-zoom, which risks missing elements during form completion.
observed_signal: Participant explained they do not want to use pinch-zoom because they miss elements (boxes, dots) when filling out forms.
stated_signal: I don't wanna pinch the Zoom because I miss you when you're filling out these forms, if you pinch the Zoom, they usually have like other boxes and dots and things that, you know, I miss.
rationale: The participant identified a conflict between two accessibility needs: the ability to read text (which requires zoom) and the ability to see all form elements at once (which zoom prevents). This represents a design accessibility failure where the interface does not support accessible form interaction patterns without forcing a choice between two critical functions, resulting in significant friction for form completion.
structural_amplification_note: This friction is structurally amplified for users with low vision or magnification software users who must choose between text legibility and form field visibility, creating an unsolvable accessibility barrier.

finding: Participant expressed uncertainty about navigation, stating 'we can't go back' before finding the program search pathway.
observed_signal: Participant navigated to 'Start an application', then moved to 'Find a program', selected 'Business' and 'Masters', and located multiple postgraduate programs (Master of Agribusiness, Master of Business, Master of Business and Law).
stated_signal: OK, so we can't go back.
rationale: The participant expressed wayfinding uncertainty ('we can't go back') but successfully navigated to the program search and discovered multiple relevant postgraduate options. The hesitation was brief and did not block task progress; the participant recovered and completed the search successfully.

finding: Participant struggled to locate the master's degree course entry point and expressed frustration with inaccessible language used in the course materials.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through the interface searching for a master's program, hesitated multiple times ('Uh, no, no. OK.'), and eventually found a course listing. Participant then paused to comment on language clarity.
stated_signal: the language doesn't feel very accessible, like very... routine for people who aren't college students.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated that course description language is not accessible to non-college students, indicating comprehension friction. The observed hesitation and search pattern combined with the stated frustration about jargon accessibility both point to noticeable but recoverable friction. The participant did eventually locate the course, matching the L2 gate (moderate friction, hesitation or non-optimal path, task completed).

finding: Participant discovered that search results were limited and did not reflect the full scope of available courses, requiring very specific search terms to get results.
observed_signal: Participant completed the search task and received results; upon reflection, noted that only 5 business results were returned despite expecting many more courses to be available.
stated_signal: They were quite strict in their responses, like it came back with 5 for business, and I'm sure there's a lot more than 5, so.
rationale: The participant's stated observation and inferred experience both indicate that available courses were not fully discoverable through the search interface. The participant expressed mild frustration that results appeared artificially constrained, though the task was ultimately completed. This represents noticeable but recoverable content discovery friction.

finding: Participant experienced friction with search specificity requirements, noting that generic searches would not work and users must be very precise in their query terms.
observed_signal: Participant completed the search successfully but reflected on the need to be 'very specific' with search terms, emphasizing that 'you can't just be, you know, hope for the best.'
stated_signal: You just had to be very specific, like you can't just be, you know, hope for the best, you have to actually put in what it is before it will come up.
rationale: The participant's statement indicates that the search system required highly specific terminology and did not support exploratory or partial queries. While the task was completed, the participant's reflection reveals a usability friction point: the system does not accommodate users who may not know the exact terminology before searching.

finding: Participant identified persistent accessibility issues with white background contrast across the website that become problematic when color inversion is applied.
observed_signal: Participant described encountering the same accessibility issues across the whole website with white background, noting the problem is exacerbated after inverting colors.
stated_signal: We're having the same accessibility issues across the whole website, the white background, so now that we've inverted the colors, this is going to be an issue because.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated a structural accessibility barrier affecting color contrast and readability that persists across multiple pages and is amplified by assistive technology use (color inversion). This is a significant component failure that impacts usability for users relying on contrast adjustments, matching S3 (significant component failure worked around or skipped).
structural_amplification_note: This friction is structurally amplified for users with low vision or colour blindness who rely on color inversion or high-contrast modes; the white background creates compound accessibility failures when assistive technologies are activated.

finding: Participant encountered a calendar interface that does not support pinch-to-zoom functionality and expressed preference for numeric input instead of calendar selection.
observed_signal: Participant attempted to interact with a calendar control, noted that it was not inverted, and commented on the inability to pinch to zoom.
stated_signal: We can't pinch to zoom. So this type of calendar is normally quite annoying. Um. Typically I'd prefer it if you can just put the numbers in and not actually have to select things on a calendar.
