The participant recovered by deciding to check RMIT and Monash individually, indicating a noticeable but recoverable hesitation that did not block task progress.

finding: Participant encountered inconsistent result counts (30 vs 54 results) and expressed confusion about why the numbers changed between searches.
observed_signal: Participant noted receiving 30 results initially, then 54 results on a subsequent search, and explicitly stated uncertainty about the discrepancy.
stated_signal: I don't know why I got 30 results, 1 to 30 or 30 before and 1 to 54 this time, but anyway.
rationale: The participant observed unexpected behaviour (inconsistent result counts) and verbally expressed confusion. This represents a brief disorientation moment that the participant worked through without abandoning the task. The alignment of observed discrepancy and stated confusion meets the S5 gate (noticeable but recoverable friction with quick recovery).

finding: Participant repeated the same course title (Master of Business Analytics) multiple times in succession while navigating results, suggesting disorientation or unclear navigation flow.
observed_signal: Participant listed 'Master of Business Analytics' with identical details three times in close succession: 'Master of Busines Analytics, 2 items, heading level 3. External. 3 years part-time...' then repeated the entry twice more with nearly identical phrasing.
rationale: The observed repetition of the same result without advancing suggests the participant either became disoriented in the list or was uncertain whether they had already reviewed that item. Without a verbal statement, the friction is inferred from behaviour alone. This pattern indicates wayfinding uncertainty at a low-to-moderate level, meeting L2.

finding: Participant expressed uncertainty about which navigation item was currently selected, indicating unclear visual feedback on the active state.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through menu items (Overview, Entry requirements, Fees and scholarships) and explicitly stated confusion about which was selected.
stated_signal: I don't know which of these is currently selected. I'm assuming overview is selected.
rationale: The participant made an explicit statement of uncertainty about interface state and had to guess which tab was active. This is wayfinding/confidence friction (F2) that caused brief hesitation but did not block task completion. The participant recovered by assuming and proceeding, matching the S5 and L2 gates.

finding: Participant restarted the search after filtering by study level and study areas, indicating confusion about filter application or results.
observed_signal: Participant applied multiple filters (study level, study areas, single/dual degrees), reviewed results, then stated 'Just gonna redo this um search. My own mistake there.' and reset to begin a new search.
stated_signal: Just gonna redo this um search. My own mistake there.
rationale: The participant's statement and action both indicate uncertainty about the filter state or results quality, prompting a restart. The participant attributed the restart to their own mistake rather than system confusion, but the behaviour shows hesitation and self-correction. This is a brief detour with recovery, matching S5 and L2.

finding: Participant received zero results after applying keyword search 'Business' with postgraduate filter, requiring a new search strategy.
observed_signal: Participant searched by keyword for 'Business', applied postgraduate filter, received zero results, then restarted the search process.
stated_signal: We said Zero results use assist getting level 3. What a surprise.
rationale: The participant encountered an empty result set and expressed surprise, indicating the outcome was unexpected. The stated tone ('What a surprise') suggests mild frustration. The participant recovered by modifying the search strategy, but the unexpected zero-result state created a brief wayfinding obstacle.

finding: Participant expressed frustration about needing clarification on UQ's non-standard terminology where degrees are called programs and subjects are called courses.
observed_signal: Participant read aloud the terminology explanation from the page and commented on it directly.
stated_signal: It's funny that I need to be given a terminology course. Article at UQ degrees are called programs and subjects are called courses.
rationale: The participant experienced comprehension friction due to non-standard terminology that required explicit clarification. The statement indicates mild irritation ('it's funny that I need to be given a terminology course'), and the participant had to read through and process this clarification before proceeding. This is recoverable friction with no task blockage, matching S5 and L2.

finding: Participant encountered confusing or garbled text while reading page content aloud, stating the course name and fee information appeared to be corrupted or mispronounced.
observed_signal: Participant read aloud 'Master of Busines Administ' and 'Master of Businessdministration' with apparent text corruption or accessibility rendering issues when vocalizing heading and content labels.
rationale: The participant's screen reader or text-to-speech output produced garbled or truncated course titles ('Busines Administ' instead of 'Business Administration'), indicating an accessibility rendering issue. The participant continued reading without explicit complaint but the corrupted text is observable evidence of accessibility friction. No explicit statement was made, so stated_signal is null.
structural_amplification_note: Assistive technology user (screen reader reliance evidenced by heading-level announcements) experienced text corruption or label truncation that would not be visible to sighted users, amplifying comprehension friction.

finding: Participant was confused by the fee structure display, uncertain whether the 8 units represented full-time or part-time study and whether the stated cost applied to their intended study load.
observed_signal: Participant read the fee information aloud, repeatedly questioned the relationship between 8 units and part-time study, stated uncertainty about cost applicability, and then decided to back up and look at a different course rather than proceed.
stated_signal: I'm a little bit confused there. Um, approximate yearly cost of tuition, 8 units, your fees will vary according to your selected courses and for 8 units. I'm doing part-time, which I would have thought was 2 units per semester. So, does that mean, does that mean it's half that? Um, as I'm not sure, I'm gonna have to actually go and look at a different course.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated confusion about how the fee structure relates to study load and part-time study. Both observed behaviour (backing up to find a different course) and stated signal (repeated questioning and expressed uncertainty) indicate comprehension friction. The participant worked around the issue by changing course rather than resolving the confusion on the current page, matching S4 (major difficulty with detour) and L3 (struggle with multiple attempts before recovery).

finding: Participant experienced confusion about part-time course pricing and load structure, noting that the displayed cost and subject count did not align with their expectations for part-time study.
observed_signal: Participant stated 'a little bit confused there' and explicitly described confusion around part-time course costs and the number of subjects per semester, noting that '4 subjects per semester may still be the um the part-time rate' but that '4700 though sounds more like a um definitely does sound like a part-time cost, if that's the, the fee for the year.'
stated_signal: a little bit confused there, um. My understanding of a part-time course I thought would be. You know, full-time was 44 subjects per semester, so the costing looked strange. 4700 though sounds more like a um definitely does sound like a part-time cost, if that's the, the fee for the year.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated confusion about terminology and pricing structure. The mismatch between expected part-time course load and displayed cost suggests unclear or inconsistent labelling of course information. The participant recovered cognitively and continued navigating, so this is noticeable but recoverable friction without task blocking.

finding: Participant experienced confusion about terminology inconsistency, questioning why the university uses 'degree', 'program', and 'subject' interchangeably when standard terminology would be clearer and more aligned with common user expectations.
observed_signal: Participant paused and explicitly questioned the terminology choice, expressing uncertainty about the labeling convention and its potential to mislead about degree level (undergraduate vs. postgraduate).
stated_signal: A little bit of a question around why I have to use different terminology from what you expect other people would know. If you think most people are going to think of it as a degree, then why not call it a degree. Um, but I guess that makes you then think it's undergraduate rather than postgraduate, I don't know. That was the only bit of language that I found unusual and sort of made me stop and think.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated confusion about the terminology choice and stopped to think about it, indicating comprehension friction. However, the participant recovered and completed the reflection without task abandonment, and acknowledged the explanation was clear overall. This represents noticeable but recoverable friction with brief hesitation and mild confusion.

finding: Participant encountered a screen reader interface that presented dense, complex legal document structure with repeated heading levels and nested definitions, making it difficult to navigate and comprehend the content.
observed_signal: Participant read aloud a series of heading levels, definitions, and nested list items from a Terms of Use page. The participant stated 'No, OK, I shouldn't try that' and attempted to interact with sidebar and toolbar elements, suggesting disorientation within the page structure.
stated_signal: No, OK, I shouldn't try that. Show sidebar of toolbar.
rationale: The participant is using a screen reader and encountered a Terms of Use page with complex nested headings, multiple heading levels, and list structures that created navigation confusion. The stated concern ('I shouldn't try that') and observed attempts to show/hide sidebar and toolbar indicate the participant struggled with the accessibility presentation of the legal content. This represents significant friction for an AT user navigating a document-heavy page.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user navigating a legally complex document with deeply nested heading hierarchy and repeated structural markers (heading level 1, 2, 3 interspersed with definitions and lists) significantly amplifies friction. The lack of clear semantic landmarks and logical reading order creates compounded difficulty for AT users.

finding: Participant attempting to navigate a registration form using keyboard controls was unable to locate form controls; keyboard navigation did not land on expected input fields.
observed_signal: Participant tried to use keyboard navigation (command/control + J) to jump between form fields but found that links and elements were not recognized as control elements, forcing them to explore the page structure through screen reader announcements.
stated_signal: I was hoping it was a button or a tick box, and I was trying to jump by form field, but there wasn't... Starting keyboard would help.
rationale: The participant's stated expectation of keyboard navigation and button/form-field jumps conflicted with the actual interface structure. The observed behaviour shows repeated attempts to navigate using expected keyboard shortcuts without success, and the stated frustration indicates significant accessibility friction. This represents a structural keyboard-navigation failure on a registration form.
structural_amplification_note: Keyboard or assistive-technology user cohort: the absence of proper keyboard navigation landmarks and the failure of expected keyboard jump commands (e.g., command/control + J) creates compounding friction for users who rely on keyboard-only or AT-assisted navigation.

finding: Participant encountered repeated accessibility errors related to form field autofill and labeling that blocked progression through the application form.
observed_signal: Participant cycled through multiple form fields (family name, email, password, program selection) encountering repeated misspelled error messages and unclear autofill instructions. The participant read aloud the same error pattern multiple times: 'To open the autofill menu, press the apple down arrow key' with variations, and encountered 'misspelled E9 doxy content' errors on the program selection dropdown.
stated_signal: What did I say? [repeated attempts to parse instructions] Do I intend to apply for check mark, please select.
rationale: The repeated misspelled error messages, unclear autofill menu instructions, and the final incomplete program selection ('Do I intend to apply for check mark, please select' with no apparent selection made) indicate accessibility friction tied to form labels, error messaging clarity, and keyboard navigation. The participant cycled through these issues multiple times without clear resolution, matching the S4/L3 gate of multiple failed attempts and substantial extra time before recovery or abandonment.
structural_amplification_note: Assistive technology user or keyboard-reliant user is experiencing amplified friction from misspelled/unclear error messages and autofill menu instructions that are not designed for screen reader or keyboard navigation clarity.

finding: Participant encountered a form validation error requiring a valid email address but the error message did not clearly explain what format was needed or how to correct it.
observed_signal: Participant attempted to submit the registration form, received a 'please enter a valid email address' error message multiple times, and repeated the same action without visible progress or correction.
rationale: The participant encountered the same validation error repeatedly without recovery shown in the window, indicating the error message did not provide sufficient guidance to resolve the issue. The repeated failed attempts and lack of visible correction path match S4 and L3 (multiple attempts, friction, task not completed in this window).

finding: Participant encountered heading markup errors and form field parsing problems while navigating the registration form with screen reader or keyboard access.
observed_signal: Participant's verbal output shows repeated parsing of malformed form elements: 'Heading not found' appears twice, email field text was read as fragmented insertions ('insertion end of text'), and checkbox labels were read as 'ticked tick box' rather than coherent instructions.
stated_signal: I could easily navigate through there with TB as well, which was good.
rationale: The observed signal shows significant accessibility defects: missing headings, malformed form field parsing, and fragmented text rendering are objective evidence of F3 friction. The stated signal expresses satisfaction with tab navigation, but the observed parsing errors indicate the form was difficult to parse and required multiple readings. Per conflict rule, severity is scored to observed behaviour (high friction, multiple attempts to parse elements) rather than the positive statement. This is S4 rather than S1 because the participant did not abandon the task and appeared to eventually submit the form.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader or keyboard-only user cohort experiences structurally higher friction due to missing heading markup and malformed form field labels/parsing, which makes form navigation and completion significantly more effortful than for sighted mouse users.

finding: Participant searched for 'Business Law' with Master's Coursework filter applied and received zero matching results, indicating a content-not-found outcome.
observed_signal: Participant navigated the University of Queensland program search interface, applied filters (Award Type: Master's Coursework, Study Type: Any), entered 'Business Law' as the keyword, and the search returned 'No matching programs'.
stated_signal: Search now about research for business law. No. Matching programs.
rationale: The participant explicitly encountered a zero-result state for a valid search query. Although the participant did not express frustration verbally, the observed inability to locate matching programs on a targeted search represents a task component blocker. The participant moved on without attempting alternative filters or search terms, suggesting the outcome blocked progression within that search pathway.

finding: Participant expressed uncertainty about whether they had completed the necessary steps and needed confirmation before proceeding further.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through the application dashboard, located the search interface with keyword and award type filters, and paused before clicking the search button, then stated uncertainty about task completion.
stated_signal: OK, and I think that's as far as I have to go. I'm not sure that um.
rationale: The participant demonstrated wayfinding uncertainty after reaching the search interface. They paused and expressed doubt about whether they had completed the task correctly, indicating brief confidence friction. The statement and hesitation align, and the participant recovered without requiring external intervention, fitting the S5/L2 gate for noticeable but recoverable friction.

finding: Participant appeared to misspell or garble a search term while attempting to enter 'Business' as a keyword.
observed_signal: Participant stated they would search for 'Master of Law with a focus on Master of Business with a focus on law' and then began typing, but the transcription shows 'Mispellbuin L S S E N I S U V' instead of a clear keyword entry.
stated_signal: So I'm just gonna search for business.
rationale: The stated intent was clear ('search for business'), but the observed input appears garbled or incorrectly transcribed, suggesting a potential input error or interface response issue. The conflicting signals (clear intent vs. unclear execution) indicate a minor friction event that did not block task progression but represents a non-optimal interaction path.

finding: Participant encountered confusing or missing navigation structure with multiple 'Heading not found' messages and uncertainty about being on the correct login page.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through a page listing documents, applications, and navigation elements, then encountered repeated 'Heading not found' announcements while trying to find the program search functionality.
stated_signal: Is it that I'm on the wrong login?
rationale: The participant's question 'Is it that I'm on the wrong login?' shows wayfinding uncertainty. The multiple 'Heading not found' structural announcements and the participant's navigation through account/documents pages before reaching the task content indicate brief disorientation. The participant recovered and located the 'Find a program' section, suggesting a recoverable but noticeable friction event.
structural_amplification_note: Participant is using a screen reader (evidenced by verbatim accessibility tree announcements). Missing or incorrectly structured headings amplify wayfinding friction for assistive-technology users, as landmark navigation becomes unreliable.

finding: Participant encountered a non-responsive browser and repeatedly attempted to search for programs using filters, resulting in no matching results across multiple search attempts.
observed_signal: Participant stated 'Safari is not responding', then repeatedly entered search terms (business law, business), selected award types and study types from dropdowns, and clicked 'Search now' multiple times, each time receiving 'no matching programs' or 'not found' messages.
stated_signal: Safari is not responding. No matching program. No. Research, search now... Hitting not found.
rationale: The browser was unresponsive at the outset, and subsequent search queries produced no visible results despite repeated attempts with different filter combinations. The participant's behaviour shows frustration through repetition and cycling through options, but the issue persists across multiple attempts without recovery or task completion. This represents significant component failure (search functionality not returning results) that the participant worked around by repeatedly trying variations.

finding: Participant experienced repeated search failures when attempting to find postgraduate programs, requiring multiple form resets and filter selections without achieving a successful result within the task window.
observed_signal: Participant cycled through selecting award types (master's by coursework, graduate diploma, doctorate by research), study types (any, multiple options), and repeatedly clicked 'Search now', encountering 'no matching programs' messages each time before resetting and trying again.
stated_signal: No matching programs... Maybe check a master... Any study type... Any award type... No matching programs.
rationale: The participant could not locate any matching programs using the search interface despite multiple attempts with varied search terms and filter combinations. While the participant did not explicitly state frustration, the observed behaviour of repeated cycles through the same workflow without success, followed by resetting and trying alternative paths, demonstrates significant friction and multiple failed attempts before recovery.

finding: Participant encountered a heading level mismatch and missing heading labels while navigating the login and application portal interface.
observed_signal: Participant read aloud: 'heading not found, agents and partners heading level 2. UQ application heading not found' and navigated through multiple heading levels and unlabeled sections including 'Agents and registered agents', 'Technical support', 'Contact', and application lists.
stated_signal: heading not found, agents and partners heading level 2. UQ application heading not found.
rationale: The participant's screen reader output explicitly reports missing headings and inconsistent heading levels, indicating accessibility friction with the page structure. The repeated 'heading not found' statements and navigational confusion through multiple unlabeled sections suggest significant accessibility barrier that required multiple attempts to parse the page hierarchy.
structural_amplification_note: This friction is structurally amplified for assistive technology users (screen reader reliance). Missing heading labels and level mismatches severely degrade the ability to build a mental model of page structure and navigate efficiently.

finding: Participant struggled to locate and understand the functional purpose of multiple interface sections including incomplete applications, submitted applications, and document access areas.
observed_signal: Participant cycled through sections labeled 'Incomplete applications', 'submitted applications', 'View', 'my documents', 'Technical support', 'Contact', and 'Copyright' without clear progression or confirmation of task completion in the assigned search task.
rationale: The participant navigated multiple application and document management sections without verbal confirmation of purpose or next steps. The repetitive cycling through similar-labeled items and the mismatch between the assigned task (discovering postgraduate course information) and the apparent login/application portal interface suggests significant wayfinding and confidence friction.

finding: Participant encountered screen reader output describing table structure and application status information without clear context, and then attempted to interact with a search field after expressing uncertainty about the interface state.
observed_signal: Participant listened to extensive accessibility tree narration including incomplete applications, submitted applications, document counts, and navigation menus. Then stated 'Let's try that again' and attempted to interact with a 'Find a program' search field, suggesting confusion about current location.
stated_signal: Let's try that again. Yes, I am.
rationale: The participant's verbal hesitation ('Let's try that again') and the attempt to re-engage with a search field suggest wayfinding uncertainty or disorientation within the interface structure. The extensive screen reader output of application management content (incomplete applications, submitted applications, documents) does not align with the task objective of discovering postgraduate course information, indicating the participant may have been unsure of their current location or how to proceed. This is moderate friction with quick recovery.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user experienced high cognitive load from extensive table and list narration without semantic landmarks to orient to task context. The dense accessibility tree output may have obscured the path to program search functionality.

finding: Participant could not understand the abbreviations and coded terminology used in the course table and explicitly stated confusion about the notation system.
observed_signal: Participant read through multiple table columns repeatedly, attempting to decode abbreviations like PGCW, MPGC, and codes, then stated explicit confusion about the terminology.
stated_signal: I don't know what that means, um. But yeah, no, I don't understand that, that terminology.
rationale: The participant encountered jargon and coded abbreviations (PGCW, MPGC, NPGC, PG Golf, etc.) that were not explained and explicitly stated lack of understanding. Multiple repeated attempts to decode the notation combined with stated confusion indicate significant comprehension friction that required substantial effort to process.

finding: Participant found the table layout for postgraduate course results significantly more difficult to navigate than the alternative heading-based layout used for degree search results.
observed_signal: Participant described struggling to interact with the table structure, noting cursor placement at end of text and difficulty finding actionable links like 'apply now' compared to the easier heading-based degree layout.
stated_signal: I don't love that the results are in a table, but it's... only because it makes it a little bit trickier to have a closer look at things. It gets a little bit tricky to navigate... I'd find that easy to use this, this table... although the table is... it's relatively accessible.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated and demonstrated that the table layout requires more work and effort than the alternative heading-based layout, requiring more steps and cognitive effort to locate and interact with the same information. Despite acknowledging the table is 'relatively accessible,' the participant conveyed noticeable friction through repeated references to increased difficulty and extra work required.
structural_amplification_note: For keyboard or screen reader users, table navigation with inconsistent cursor placement and distributed link targets may amplify the effort friction beyond what visual users experience.

finding: Participant expressed difficulty with screen reader compatibility of the search results layout compared to an alternative presentation.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through search results using screen reader, then verbally compared the current layout unfavorably to a different format.
stated_signal: Just probably not quite as nice to use with a screen reader as just laid out.
rationale: The participant stated that the search results layout is less accessible with a screen reader than an alternative would be. The participant continued through the task without blocking, but explicitly noted a usability gap for assistive technology users. This constitutes accessibility friction with a brief negative sentiment and recovery.
structural_amplification_note: Participant is using a screen reader; the current layout structure presents reduced usability for AT users compared to the alternative mentioned, representing structural amplification of accessibility friction.

finding: Participant encountered unexpected behaviour when attempting to enter a date using a date picker interface, requiring multiple attempts with spinner controls instead of direct typing.
observed_signal: Participant attempted to type numbers directly into the date field, then repeatedly used month/day/year stepper controls, cycling through values multiple times (e.g., '0 month', 'step up', '2026, 2027, 2023, 2020, 2019, 1996, 1995, 1999').
stated_signal: That didn't work the way I expected it to. So it's funny that just typing in numbers didn't seem to work.
rationale: The participant expected direct numeric input to work but the interface defaulted to spinner controls, forcing multiple failed attempts and workarounds. Both observed repeated cycling and stated surprise ('didn't work the way I expected', 'funny that just typing didn't work') confirm significant friction. The participant eventually cycled through enough values to recover, matching S4 (major difficulty with multiple attempts before recovery) and L3 (significant friction, struggle with multiple attempts).

finding: Participant experienced significant accessibility friction with screen reader announcement of form controls, particularly with a pop-up button that did not announce correctly, requiring manual workarounds.
observed_signal: Participant reported that the screen reader was not reading out content while arrowing through controls; attempted to type 'MR' as a workaround; stated 'not ideal that that pop-up button didn't work' and 'not ideal at all'; navigated through multiple form fields with repeated focus loss and manual control selection.
stated_signal: It's not reading out what I'm arrowing down through. Not ideal that that pop-up button didn't work. Not ideal at all. Ah, again, focus has gone somewhere.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated that the screen reader was not announcing content and that focus management was failing repeatedly. The observed behaviour shows multiple workarounds (typing instead of using controls, manual selection of radio buttons) and expressions of frustration. This matches S4 (major difficulty with multiple failed attempts and workarounds) and L3 (significant friction with struggle and frustration, though the form was ultimately completed).
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader or assistive technology user experienced structurally amplified friction due to unlabeled controls, missing announcements, and focus management failures that directly impede keyboard and AT navigation.

finding: Participant encountered repeated focus loss and unexpected focus behaviour during form navigation, requiring manual refocusing and re-selection of controls.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through form fields and stated 'Ah, again, focus has gone somewhere'; repeatedly had to re-interact with radio buttons (e.g., 'Interact with the title of radio button', 'Stop interacting'); cycled through multiple radio button states multiple times before achieving the desired selection.
stated_signal: Ah, again, focus has gone somewhere.
rationale: The interface did not maintain focus as expected during form navigation; focus moved to unexpected locations, requiring the participant to manually correct it multiple times. The stated signal confirms frustration with this unexpected behaviour. The multiple corrections and repeated attempts to select controls match S4 and L3 (significant friction with multiple failed attempts before recovery).

finding: Screen reader is not announcing expanded combo box options when the participant arrows through them, preventing accessible navigation of the preferred title dropdown.
observed_signal: Participant expanded the preferred title combo box (14 items listed), arrowed up and down, but stated the screen reader is not reading anything out; participant then abandoned interaction with this control.
stated_signal: I'm arrowing up and down there, maybe that's visible, but the screen reader's not reading anything out.
rationale: The participant is using a screen reader (assistive technology) and explicitly reported that expanded menu options are not being read aloud despite the control showing 14 items. This is a direct accessibility failure; the participant worked around it by skipping the control, meeting the S3 gate (significant component failure or barrier worked around).
structural_amplification_note: Blind or low-vision users relying on screen reader output cannot access the expanded combo box list when options are not announced, creating a complete barrier to using this control with assistive technology.

finding: Screen reader does not announce expanded options in the gender combo box, forcing the participant to use a text-input workaround (typing 'MA' to filter) instead of navigating the menu.
observed_signal: Participant expanded the gender combo box (5 items), stated it is 'not working at all', then typed 'MA' to narrow the list rather than using keyboard navigation; eventually male was selected.
stated_signal: Again, not working at all.
rationale: The participant encountered the same screen reader failure on a second combo box (5 items not announced), expressed frustration ('not working at all'), and used a non-standard text-input workaround to proceed. This is repeated accessibility friction requiring a detour; the component task was completed but with significant extra effort and cognitive load.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users cannot use keyboard menu navigation when expanded options are not announced, forcing them to discover and use alternative input methods (typing to filter) instead of standard accessible patterns.

finding: Date of birth field rejected valid input (17 February 2026) with an 'invalid data' error message, and the participant struggled to determine the correct format despite multiple attempts.
observed_signal: Participant entered '17th of February 2026' in the date field; system returned 'invalid data' error; participant then attempted alternative formats ('1's 19th Thursday', '1's 3 Tuesday', '1's 19th Thursday', '17th January') without clear recovery or clarity on the correct format.
rationale: The date field rejected the participant's input and provided no guidance on the required format. The participant made multiple attempts with different phrasings, suggesting uncertainty about what format the system expects. This meets the S4 gate (multiple failed attempts and significant extra cognitive effort before recovery).

finding: Participant struggled with an unintuitive date entry interface, requiring multiple attempts and tabbing through controls before successfully entering the date.
observed_signal: Participant attempted to type the date directly, then used keyboard tabbing and step controls (step up/step down) multiple times to navigate and enter 2 February 1989, eventually succeeding after several cycles.
stated_signal: Not that intuitive, but once I get focus right, it is work.
rationale: The participant's initial expectation to type directly was not met, forcing reliance on step controls and tabbing. The statement acknowledges low intuitiveness, and the observed multiple attempts and cycles indicate significant friction and uncertainty about the correct interaction method, matching the L3 gate (significant friction, multiple attempts, task completed).
structural_amplification_note: Keyboard or assistive-technology reliance amplified friction with the step-control interface for date entry.

finding: Participant was uncertain about what would be selected when tabbing through form controls without clear labeling or focus indicators.
observed_signal: Participant expressed doubt mid-navigation: 'by tabbing, I don't know what I'm gonna be selected' and noted confusion about which radio button or control would receive focus.
stated_signal: So just by tabbing, I don't know what I'm gonna be selected.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated uncertainty about keyboard focus order and which control would be selected when tabbing. This is a core accessibility friction issue (F3) related to focus management and labeling clarity. The participant recovered by continuing to navigate, but the friction was significant and explicitly acknowledged.
structural_amplification_note: Keyboard users or assistive-technology users are structurally amplified by unclear focus order and lack of visible focus indicators on form controls.

finding: Participant encountered persistent combo box malfunction when selecting state/territory, explicitly stating the controls are not working well.
observed_signal: Participant attempted to interact with the state/territory combo box, which did not respond as expected; the participant then moved through subsequent form fields including address, suburb, and postcode entry.
stated_signal: combo boxes are not working well at all.
rationale: The participant explicitly reported that combo boxes are not working well, and the observed behaviour shows repeated interaction attempts with the control without successful state selection. This represents a significant accessibility or functionality barrier that required the participant to work around or continue despite the malfunction, matching the S4 and L3 gates (major difficulty and multiple failed attempts before recovery or workaround).
structural_amplification_note: If the participant is using a keyboard or screen reader, combo box accessibility defects (missing ARIA labels, incorrect focus management, or unresponsive keyboard navigation) would structurally amplify friction for assistive-technology users.

finding: Participant struggled with autofill menu interactions across multiple address fields, requiring explicit instructions to navigate and enter data.
observed_signal: Participant interacted with Address Line 1, Address Line 2, and Suburb/Town fields, each with autofill menus; the transcript shows repeated instruction reminders (e.g., 'press the apple down arrow key, then press the return key') and apparent difficulty with field selection and data entry (evidenced by garbled or repeated entries such as 'Jeff 111 Jeff', 'Paul', 'Pull', 'Paul Smith').
rationale: The observed signals show repeated failed attempts, confusion with autofill menu navigation, and apparent data entry errors across multiple form fields. The need for repeated instruction cues and the pattern of garbled or corrected entries indicate high friction and multiple recovery attempts, matching S4 (major difficulty) and L3 (significant friction with multiple attempts). No verbal statement is available, so sentiment and stated_signal remain null.
structural_amplification_note: Keyboard or screen reader users would face compounded difficulty with autofill menu focus management and announce­ment timing; the repeated instruction reminders suggest the autofill pattern is not self-evident, particularly for users relying on assistive technology.

finding: Participant repeatedly cycled through dropdown menu selections for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander status without successfully progressing, expressing uncertainty about the correct option.
observed_signal: Participant opened and closed the dropdown menu multiple times, selected different options (Aboriginal, Torres Strait Islander, both Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander), pressed down to open menus repeatedly, and cycled back through the same field without advancing to the next step.
stated_signal: Give me what I wanted, I don't think. Alright, if I go right to the last one. What is that?
rationale: The participant's repeated attempts to select the correct option, combined with the stated doubt ('I don't think [this is] what I wanted'), indicates significant wayfinding and comprehension friction. Multiple failed attempts to progress past this single field, with no clear advancement, demonstrate struggle matching the S4/L3 gate (multiple attempts before recovery or abandonment).
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader or assistive technology users may experience amplified friction due to verbose or repetitive menu announcements during cycling, as evidenced by the dense accessibility tree narration in the transcript.

finding: Participant experienced inconsistent labeling and field descriptions when tabbing through form controls, sometimes receiving field information and sometimes not.
observed_signal: Participant navigated element-by-element through the page, tabbing through multiple form fields (citizenship, location, Aboriginal/Torres Strait Islander status, program of study, address fields, country code, telephone number, USI).
